chunk_summary: This section discusses general restrictions in managing decompensated cirrhosis, ascites, and pediatric liver disease, with a focus on nutrition and clinical guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of dietary and fluid restrictions in managing decompensated cirrhosis and ascites.
Identify key nutritional strategies for children with chronic liver disease.
Review clinical guidelines for managing spontaneous bacterial peritonitis and hepatorenal syndrome.
Analyze the importance of evidence-based practices in pediatric liver disease management.
key_facts: Decompensated cirrhosis often requires sodium restriction to <2 g/day to manage ascites (EASL guidelines).
Fluid restriction is recommended in cases of severe hyponatremia (serum sodium <125 mmol/L).
Nutritional support in children with chronic liver disease should include 120-150% of the recommended daily caloric intake (NASPGHAN/ESPGHAN).
Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) is a common complication in cirrhosis, requiring prompt antibiotic treatment (AASLD guidance).
Protein restriction is generally not recommended in cirrhosis unless hepatic encephalopathy is severe.
reference_quote: Nutritional support of children with chronic liver disease should aim for 120-150% of the recommended daily caloric intake to prevent malnutrition (NASPGHAN/ESPGHAN).
